content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

November 26, 1991


Jayne W. Williams
City Attorney
City of Oakland
City Hall
One City Hall Plaza
Oakland, CA 94612

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-91-513

Dear Ms. Williams:

This is in response to your letter requesting advice under
the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the "Act").1 The devastating fire in the Oakland Hills area of
the City of Oakland has resulted in the loss of homes of many
public officials who are concerned about the financial effect of
decisions they are called upon to make or participate in
concerning the rebuilding efforts that are now underway. As I
informed you at our meeting with Mayor Harris in Oakland at the
time I personally surveyed the affected area, the Act is not
intended to be a stumbling block in your rebuilding efforts. In
particular, I discussed with you the application of the "public
generally" aspect of the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions. I
hope this letter provides you with clear guidelines to enable you
to proceed with your rebuilding efforts.2

As you know, a public official may not make, participate in
making, or attempt to influence any decision which will have a
reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on, among

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 We are treating this letter as a request for informal
assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c). Informal assistance
does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an
opinion or formal written advice. (Government Code Section 83114;
2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Our File No. I-91-513
Page 2


other things, the official's real property interests.³ In your
letter you have described the real property interests of Oakland
city officials and the proximity of their real property interests

³ Section 87100 provides:

    No public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in any way
attempt to use his official position to influence a
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know
he has a financial interest.

                                    Section 87100.

Section 87103 provides:

    An official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally, on the official or a member of his or her
immediate family or on:

        (a) Any business entity in which the public official
has a direct or indirect investment worth one thousand
dollars ($1,000) or more.

        (b) Any real property in which the public official has
a direct or indirect interest worth one thousand dollars
($1,000) or more.

        (c) Any source of income, other than gifts and other
than loans by a commercial lending institution in the regular
course of business on terms available to the public without
regard to official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, received by or
promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the
time when the decision is made.

        (d) Any business entity in which the public official is
a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any
position of management.

        (e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a
donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, received by, or
promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the
time when the decision is made.

    For purposes of this section, indirect investment or
interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse
or dependent child of a public official, by an agent on
behalf of a public official, or by a business entity or trust

Our File No. I-91-513
Page 3


to the fire-damaged area.  You also advised us that as a result of
the conflagration, the City of Oakland was declared a national
disaster area by President Bush, and a state of emergency was
proclaimed by Governor Pete Wilson.  Because of the large number
of public officials whose residences may be materially affected by
decisions made in connection with the rebuilding efforts, and the
broad scope of their responsibilities in formulating the plans and
procedures for such rebuilding efforts, you advised us that the
city has been hindered in its efforts to generate the plans and
procedures to begin rebuilding the fire-damaged area. I advised
you at our meeting, and am confirming the advice by this letter,
that, for purposes of rebuilding after the Oakland fire disaster,
public officials who have residences in the fire area are not
prohibited from participating in governmental decisions as a
result of the ownership of their residences because the effect of
their decisions is the same as the effect on a significant segment
of the public.

     As we discussed, and your letter points out, the financial
effects of the fire extend beyond the boundaries of the fire-
damaged area.  It would appear that the effect of the decisions
made in connection with the rebuilding efforts will similarly
extend beyond the boundaries of the fire-damaged area, and will
affect homeowners in this extended area in a substantially similar
manner.

     We have in the past attempted to ascertain whether the
segment of the public affected by a decision constituted a
significant segment of the public for purposes of Regulation 18703
(public generally), based upon either the percentage of the public
or the number of people affected in a substantially similar
manner.  We have not addressed the issue of "public generally" in
the context of a large number of public officials residing in an
area of a jurisdiction that has been devastated by a disaster, as
in Oakland.  Recognizing that for the process of rebuilding to

_____________________________
in which the official, the official's agents, spouse, and
dependent children own directly, indirectly, or beneficially
a 10-percent interest or greater.

                                                  Section 87103.

18703.   Effect on the Public Generally

          A material financial effect of a governmental decision
on an official's interests, as described in Government Code
Section 87103, is distinguishable from its effect on the
public generally unless the decision will affect the
official's interest in substantially the same manner as it
will affect all members of the public or a significant
public....

                    * * *

Our File No. I-91-513
Page 4


start, public officials in the jurisdiction will inevitably make
decisions that affect the residences of the people in the fire-
damaged area, including the surrounding areas. I can understand
your desire for a general rule which will permit public officials
with residences in the fire-damaged area to make, participate in
making, and attempt to influence governmental decisions.

You have stated that approximately 5% of all single family
homes in the city were burned, and that the fire-damaged area
comprised 5% of Oakland's land area. As noted above, the
homeowners in the areas surrounding the fire-damaged area have
also been affected by the fire. Thus, the number of owners of
single family residences that will be affected by the rebuilding
decisions in a substantially similar manner is significantly
greater than the 5% of homeowners in the fire-damaged area, and
will constitute a significant segment of the public. Accordingly,
public officials who have homes in the affected areas are not
disqualified from making or participating in making decisions
which will have a material financial effect on their real property
interest - their residence - since the effect is the same as the
effect on a significant segment of the public.

I would add a note of caution that this "public generally"
exception permits a public official to make, participate in
making, attempt to influence a decision only if the decision
does not affect the public official in a unique manner. For
example, if a decision involves the creation of a park across the
street or next to the public official's residence, it is unlikely
that the effect of such a decision on the official will be the
same as the effect on a significant segment of the public.

I trust this letter responds to your questions and that the
Act will not hinder your rebuilding efforts. If you have any
other problems under the Act, please let me know. If you have any
questions regarding this letter, please contact Jeevan Ahuja,
Staff Counsel, at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Ben Davidian
Chairman

BD:JA:aa
